course.title: Learning to Design
course.subtitle: Documenting my step-by-step journey improving my design criteria — from fundamentals to real decisions in real projects.

chapters.title: Understanding Visual Hierarchy
chapters.description: How I learned to guide the eye through a layout — size, contrast, spacing, and the invisible structure behind every good design.

chapter01.label: Chapter 01
chapter01.title: Understanding Visual Hierarchy
chapter01.intro1: When I started designing, everything felt flat. I'd place elements on a page and nothing stood out — or everything did. The turning point was understanding that design is about directing attention. Visual hierarchy is how you tell someone where to look first, second, and third.
chapter01.intro2: In this chapter, I'll walk through the core principles I learned and the prompts and tools I used along the way to build my intuition.
chapter01.section1Text: Before touching any tool, I asked Claude to help me understand the fundamentals. This single prompt changed how I approach every layout.
chapter01.section1Code: Explain visual hierarchy in web design. What are the 3 most important principles a beginner should learn first? Give me real examples I can practice.
chapter01.section2Title: Applying contrast to my own layout
chapter01.section2Text: Once I understood the theory, I needed to test it. I opened my portfolio and used this prompt to get a critique of my own hero section.
chapter01.section2Code: Review this hero section for visual hierarchy. Is the heading dominant enough? Does the subtitle feel secondary? What would you change to improve the contrast between elements?
chapter01.section3Title: The spacing trick that changed everything
chapter01.section3Text1: One thing I kept getting wrong was spacing. Everything was either crammed together or floating in empty space. The breakthrough was learning that spacing creates grouping — elements that are close together feel related, and generous space between groups creates natural separation.
chapter01.section3Text2: I started using a simple CSS command to audit my own spacing decisions in the browser:
chapter01.section4Text1: Visual hierarchy isn't about making things bigger or bolder — it's about making intentional decisions about what matters most. Every element on a page competes for attention. Your job as a designer is to make sure the right things win.
chapter01.section4Text2: The three principles I keep coming back to: size creates importance, contrast creates focus, and spacing creates relationships. Once you see it, you can't unsee it.

about.quote: Curiosity-first. I research, design, and execute until complexity becomes clarity.
